cards.title: Entering a Subway Car at Night
cards.description: It’s 10:45 PM. A train arrives. One car is empty; the next is moderately full.
do: Choose the car with people
Keep your bag in front
Stay aware at each stop
dont: Don’t sit in the far corner
cards.anchor: Most NYC rides are uneventful — choosing a populated car keeps you safe.

cards.title: Someone is acting erratic on the train
cards.description: A person is pacing, shouting, or behaving unpredictably.
do: Move calmly to another car
Stand near the door
Avoid eye contact
cards.anchor: Changing cars is normal in NYC — riders do it all the time.

cards.title: Late‑Night Transfer in a Quiet Station
cards.description: You need to switch lines at a station with low foot traffic.
do: Stay on the main platform
Stand near the Help Point intercom
Move toward groups or well‑lit areas
dont: Don’t stand alone at the far end
cards.anchor: Staying in visible, central areas is the safest choice at night.

cards.title: Walking Past a Group on the Sidewalk
cards.description: A group is gathered ahead, taking up most of the sidewalk.
do: Cross the street early if possible
Walk with purpose and steady pace
Keep your phone accessible but not out
cards.anchor: Changing your path early is normal in NYC — locals do it constantly.

cards.title: Someone Following You for a Block
cards.description: You notice someone behind you matching your pace.
do: Change direction or cross the street
Enter a store or well‑lit area
Call someone so your voice is heard
cards.anchor: Redirecting your route is a powerful way to break unwanted patterns.

cards.title: Crowded Times Square Encounter
cards.description: A costumed character or promoter approaches you aggressively.
do: Keep walking without stopping
Hold your bag close
Use firm but neutral body language
dont: Don’t take photos unless you want to pay
Don’t let them block your path
cards.anchor: Times Square performers rely on engagement — ignoring them is expected.

cards.title: Late‑Night Rideshare Pickup
cards.description: You’re waiting for an Uber or Lyft outside a bar or restaurant.
do: Verify the license plate before approaching
Ask the driver to confirm your name
Sit in the back seat on the passenger side
dont: Don’t get into a car with mismatched details
Don’t wait alone in a dark spot
cards.anchor: Verification is your strongest safety tool — drivers expect it.

cards.title: Unlicensed Taxi Offers a Ride
cards.description: A driver in an unmarked car waves you over.
do: Ignore and keep walking
Use only official apps or yellow cabs
Move toward a populated area
dont: Don’t get into any unmarked vehicle
cards.anchor: Licensed NYC drivers never solicit riders — ignoring is the norm.

cards.title: Drop‑Off in a Quiet Area
cards.description: Your rideshare drops you off on a dimly lit street.
do: Ask the driver to stop closer to a lit corner
Scan your surroundings before exiting
Walk confidently toward your destination
dont: Don’t walk with your head down
cards.anchor: Drivers are used to adjusting drop‑off points — it’s okay to ask.

cards.title: Lost in a Quiet Neighborhood
cards.description: You realize you’ve taken a wrong turn in a low‑traffic area.
do: Move toward the nearest lit intersection
Use your phone discreetly to re‑route
Walk with purpose even if unsure
dont: Don’t stop in the middle of the street
Don’t ask random strangers for directions
cards.anchor: Projecting confidence reduces unwanted attention dramatically.

cards.title: Someone Blocking Your Path
cards.description: A person steps into your walking line intentionally.
do: Change direction immediately
Move toward a store or group
Use your voice if needed: 'No, thank you.'
cards.anchor: Changing direction is a powerful boundary — use it early.

cards.title: Late‑Night Convenience Store Stop
cards.description: You enter a bodega or deli late at night with few customers.
do: Stand near the counter or staff
Keep your bag zipped and close
Buy quickly and leave confidently
dont: Don’t wander deep into the aisles
Don’t stay if something feels off
cards.anchor: Positioning yourself near staff increases safety and visibility.
